mūla.devanāgarī: अनादि-मध्यान्तम् अनन्त-वीर्यम् अनन्त-बाहुं शशि-सूर्य-नेत्रम् | पश्यामि त्वां दीप्त-हुताश-वक्त्रं स्व-तेजसा विश्वम् इदं तपन्तम्
mūla.iast: anādi-madhyāntam ananta-vīryam ananta-bāhuṃ śaśi-sūrya-netram | paśyāmi tvāṃ dīpta-hutāśa-vaktraṃ sva-tejasā viśvam idaṃ tapantam
mūla.chapter_position: Chapter 11 (Viśvarūpa-Darśana-Yoga (The Yoga of the Vision of the Universal Form)), verse 19
mūla.speaker: Krishna

word_by_word.grammar: accusative masculine singular present participle verb

advaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
advaita.english_rendering: Arjuna beholds the one Reality that has no origin (ādi), no middle (madhya), no end (anta) — the very structure of time is absent in what is seen, because what is seen is not a temporal object but the substrate of time itself. The infinite power (ananta-vīrya) and infinite arms (ananta-bāhu) are not enumerable attributes added to a substance; Śaṅkara reads each compound as denying any limit external to the Whole: no boundary of potency exists, no boundary of reach exists. The moon and sun are the two eyes (śaśi-sūrya-netra); the blazing fire-face (dīpta-hutāśa-vaktra) is one more limb of this limitless configuration, and the entire universe (viśva) is scorched (tapyate) by the self-luminosity (sva-tejas) of that which needs no external fuel — jñāna burns from within.
advaita.divergence_note: Śaṅkarācārya: 'ādiś ca madhyaṃ ca antaś ca na vidyate yasya saḥ ayam anādimadyānantaḥ' — the compound is parsed as triple negation of temporal markers; vīrya likewise has 'na antaḥ asti' — no limit; the self-luminosity (sva-tejas) needs no commentary because it is non-relational.

viśiṣṭādvaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
viśiṣṭādvaita.english_rendering: Arjuna sees the Bhagavān whose body is the universe: beginningless, endlessly potent (ananta-vīrya glossed by Rāmānuja as anavadhikātiśaya-jñāna-bala-aiśvarya-śakti-tejas — unbounded, unsurpassable knowledge, strength, sovereignty, power, and splendor), with countless limbs as upward extensions from a single cosmic waist — no contradiction, because the divine body is not constrained by the geometry of a finite form. The moon-eyes and sun-eyes (śaśi-sūrya-netra) carry a relational meaning: prasāda (grace, moonlike coolness) toward the devoted, pratāpa (sovereign heat) toward the demonic — the same visual organ that blesses the aligned scorches the contrary, as the subsequent verse will confirm. The entire cosmos is pervaded and ordered (tapantam) by Bhagavān's own radiance, and Arjuna sees this not merely as vision but as direct perception (sākṣātkāra) of the instruction received.
viśiṣṭādvaita.divergence_note: Rāmānuja: 'anavadhikātiśayavīryam — vīryaśabdaḥ pradarśanārthaḥ' — 'vīrya' is synecdoche for the full six-fold sovereignty; 'prasāda-pratāpa-yukta-sarva-netram' — the eye carries dual register; 'svakīyena tejasā viśvam idaṃ tapantam' — the tapana is the cosmic ordering function of Bhagavān's inherent luminosity.

dvaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
dvaita.english_rendering: The verse is not mere poetic description: Madhva anchors it to the Ṛgveda-khila testimony that all the gods (surādayaḥ) are limbs-born-from-Him (tad-aṅga-jāḥ), and moon and sun are specifically His eyes — not metaphor but ontological derivation confirmed by the Puruṣasūkta ('candramā manasaḥ jātaḥ, cakṣoḥ sūryo ajāyata'). This means the śaśi-sūrya-netra clause is not anthropomorphic imagery; it is a statement about dependent origination: the luminaries exist because they were generated from the divine eye-locus, and they continue to function as the cognizing instruments of Viṣṇu's own omniscience — distinct from Him yet wholly dependent. The infinite multiplicity of forms (bahu-rūpatva) entails that many dependent beings can have Him as their āśraya (support), which Madhva reads as vindicating the eternal plurality of jīvas.
dvaita.divergence_note: Madhva: 'ahahaṃ kratuḥ [9.16] ityādivat' — this verse repeats the same strategy of Vedic citation confirming that the apparent description is ontological identity-through-dependence; Ṛk 8.4.19.3 / Yajus 31.12 cited directly; 'bahu-rūpatvād bahv-āśrayatvaṃ teṣāṃ yuktam' — multiplicity of forms justifies multiplicity of dependent supports.

śuddhādvaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
śuddhādvaita.english_rendering: Vallabha reads the entire configuration as the Kālasvarūpa (time-form) of Kṛṣṇa manifesting for Arjuna's perception: the verse is ādhidaivika, not merely ādhyātmika — it concerns cosmic temporal functions, not abstract metaphysics. Moon and sun are the abhimāni-devatās (presiding deities) of time — Kāla's own eyes — so śaśi-sūrya-netra means Kṛṣṇa's vision operates through cosmic time itself. The blazing fire-face (dīpta-hutāśa-vaktra) carries the sāyaṃ-kālābhimāna — the twilight/evening fire that is the third temporal marker — linking creation (adi) and dissolution (anta) to the diurnal cycle. This is not terror but the rapture of seeing Kṛṣṇa's own prasāda expressing itself as the ordered rhythms of time: His līlā is the cosmos pulsing.
śuddhādvaita.divergence_note: Vallabha: 'kālarūpam etad ādhidaivikaṃ tad āha śaśi-sūrya-netram iti — kālābhimāninau śaśi-sūryau netre yasya; tathā sāyaṃ-kālābhimānī dīptaḥ hutāśaś ca vaktreṣu yasya' — the three temporal deities (moon, sun, evening-fire) are identified as facial organs of Kṛṣṇa's Kāla-body.

bhakti.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
bhakti.english_rendering: Śrīdhara's commentary is philological-devotional: the compound anādi-madhyānta is glossed precisely as 'utpatti-sthiti-pralaya-rahitam' — free from arising, sustaining, and dissolution — the three cosmic functions that belong to Brahman, not to any produced object. The verse thus tells the devotee: what you are seeing is not a cosmic spectacle external to you; you are seeing the one who is prior to all three phases of the universe's career. Infinite power, infinite arms, moon and sun as eyes, fire-face, and scorching radiance — each phrase layers the perception of inexhaustibility: the devotee's awe is proportionate to the recognition that no attribute of Bhagavān has a ceiling. The tepana (scorching) by His own radiance (sva-tejas) is the devotionally active present-tense: He is doing this now, continuously, to the entire cosmos.
bhakti.divergence_note: Śrīdhara: 'anādimadyāntam utpatti-sthiti-pralaya-rahitam' — three-fold negation; 'anantaṃ vīryaṃ prabhāvo yasya' — vīrya glossed as prabhāva (sovereign efficacy); 'sva-tejasā idaṃ viśvaṃ tapantaṃ saṃtāpayantaṃ paśyāmi' — the present-active participle tapantam is devotionally live, not static.

advaita-bhakti.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
advaita-bhakti.english_rendering: Madhusūdana tracks Śrīdhara closely on the triple compound ('ādir utpattiḥ madhyaṃ sthitiḥ anto vināśaḥ') but adds a crucial gloss on the ananta-bāhu clause: 'upalaḥkṣaṇam etan mukhādīnām api' — the infinite arms are synecdoche for all infinite limbs including face, etc. This move is simultaneously jñāna (each finite attribute points to the infinite, no part is truly bounded) and bhakti (the beloved's form keeps generating new surfaces for contemplation — the enumeration never exhausts). Arjuna's seeing (paśyāmi) is the culmination of bhakti-yoga's fruit: the devotee who has surrendered vision itself receives the viśvarūpa as darśana, which for Madhusūdana is simultaneously the direct cognition of nirguṇa Brahman and the loving sight of sa-guṇa Kṛṣṇa, held without collapse into either pole.
advaita-bhakti.divergence_note: Madhusūdana: 'upalaḥkṣaṇam etan mukhādīnām api' — synecdoche for infinite limbs; 'sva-tejasā viśvam idaṃ tapantaṃ saṃtāpayantaṃ tvā tvāṃ paśyāmi' — the self-luminosity language preserves the Advaita reading; the present-tense paśyāmi carries the bhakti weight.

corpus_provenance.mūla: Belvalkar critical edition (BORI 1947), via Ambuda multi-witness

so_what_questions: If Bhagavān is anādi-madhyānta (without beginning, middle, or end), what does it mean to 'meet' or 'encounter' Him — is encounter possible with what has no temporal boundary?
Moon and sun are read as both cosmic eyes and as grace-and-wrath — does the same perception that blesses one beholder scorch another, and what determines which?
All six schools agree the verse describes infinite potency (ananta-vīrya) — do they agree on what 'potency' means, or are they pointing at different underlying realities?
The fire-face (dīpta-hutāśa-vaktra) appears in the same verse as the cosmic scorching (viśvam tapantam) — is destruction here presented as a flaw, a function, or a form of grace?

Rāmānuja inserts a detailed spatial geometry (limbs extending above and below a single cosmic waist) to resolve the apparent contradiction of one body with infinite limbs — what work does that geometry do theologically?
Madhva grounds the moon-and-sun-eyes in Vedic cosmogony (Puruṣasūkta) — what changes when cosmological description becomes ontological derivation rather than metaphor?
Arjuna says 'I see' (paśyāmi) — is this an act of perception, recognition, or devotional reception, and does your tradition treat those as the same or as stages?

everyday_applications.advaita: When you encounter a situation that seems to have no way out — no beginning of a solution, no middle ground, no ending in sight — that very structure of 'no exit' points to what is prior to all exits: the awareness that contains the situation but is not trapped in it. Practice: when stuck, ask not 'how do I get out?' but 'what in me was never in?'
everyday_applications.viśiṣṭādvaita: The same Bhagavān whose eyes are moon and sun shows prasāda (cooling grace) to the surrendered and pratāpa (scorching authority) to the resistant. In your relationships, notice that the most loving authority figures — parents, teachers, mentors — do both: their warmth and their challenge come from the same care. Practice kainkarya (service-orientation) rather than self-assertion, and the prasāda register becomes more accessible.
everyday_applications.dvaita: Every capacity you have — intelligence, strength, creativity — is a 'limb born from Him' (tad-aṅga-ja), as the Ṛgveda says of the sun and moon. You did not generate your own abilities; they are derived from Hari's inexhaustible reservoir. Use your talents with the awareness that you are holding them in trust, not ownership. This is not self-deprecation; it is accurate accounting.
everyday_applications.śuddhādvaita: Vallabha reads the verse as Kṛṣṇa's Kāla-body — time itself is His face. When the day ends, that is the fire-face of evening. When the month turns, that is the moon-eye. When the year burns hot, that is His solar gaze. Living inside Kṛṣṇa's līlā means the rhythms of time are not neutral background noise — they are His prasāda in motion. Practice: greet each transition of time (dawn, dusk, new month) as a moment of His self-disclosure.
everyday_applications.bhakti: Śrīdhara's gloss 'utpatti-sthiti-pralaya-rahitam' — free from arising, sustaining, and dissolution — describes Bhagavān, but it also describes what devotion points toward: a relationship that does not depend on favorable conditions arising, good conditions sustaining, or bad conditions ending. Cultivate bhakti that holds steady across all three phases of any life-situation, because the object of bhakti is prior to all three.
everyday_applications.advaita-bhakti: Madhusūdana's key move is synecdoche: the infinite arms stand for all infinite limbs. In your contemplative practice, any one quality of Bhagavān — His beauty, His wisdom, His power — is a doorway to the infinite. You do not need to enumerate everything; one attribute, apprehended fully, opens into limitlessness. Begin with the quality of Kṛṣṇa that moves you most, and let the infinite unfold from that single thread.
